partI.instructions: Choose the single best answer for each question.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about computing innovations are consistent with the CED? I. A computing innovation includes a program as an integral part of its function. II. A computing innovation must be a physical object that users can touch. III. A concept such as online ride-matching, which has no physical form of its own, can still be a computing innovation.
options: I and II only
I and III only
II and III only
I, II, and III
questions.rationale: Statement I restates CRD-1.A.1: a computing innovation includes a program as an integral part of its function. Statement III matches CRD-1.A.2: an innovation can be a nonphysical computing concept, like e-commerce or ride-matching. Statement II contradicts CRD-1.A.2, which allows physical, nonphysical software, and nonphysical concept innovations. A is wrong: it includes false statement II and omits true statement III. C is wrong: it includes false statement II and omits true statement I. D is wrong: it includes false statement II.

questions.stem: The four developers of MuralMatch, an app that lets residents vote on designs for neighborhood murals, are split: two insist the voting page should display artist names, and two insist votes must stay anonymous. In a structured discussion, each side states what it would take to accept the other design, and the team settles on a version everyone supports: names appear only after voting closes. Which interpersonal skills does this discussion BEST illustrate?
options: Divide-and-conquer scheduling and fully independent work assignments
Storyboarding and creation of user-interface layout diagrams
Rapid prototyping followed by beta testing with residents
Negotiation, conflict resolution, and consensus building
questions.rationale: CRD-1.C.1 lists communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, and negotiation as interpersonal skills of effective collaborative teams; stating acceptance conditions is negotiation, resolving the split is conflict resolution, and agreeing on one version everyone supports is consensus building. A is wrong: dividing work to avoid interaction is not an interpersonal skill and avoids the disagreement instead of resolving it. B is wrong: storyboarding and layout diagrams are design-phase activities (CRD-2.F.7), not interpersonal skills. C is wrong: prototyping and testing are development-process phases, not the skills used in the team's discussion.

questions.stem: Two programmers in different cities build HarborNotes, a tide-journal app, by connecting to the same live shared code editor. One writes code while the other reviews every line as it is typed, and they trade roles each session. Which statement BEST explains how this arrangement supports their collaboration?
options: It combines an online collaboration tool with the pair-programming model, letting them share ideas and give feedback on the code in real time
It removes any need for written documentation, since both programmers watch the code as it is being written
It guarantees that the finished program will contain no syntax errors before it is ever run by a computer
It replaces the testing phase of development, since reviewing code line by line is equivalent to running defined test inputs
questions.rationale: CRD-1.B.1 says online tools support collaboration by letting programmers share and give feedback on ideas and documents, and CRD-1.B.2 names pair programming as a common collaboration model; the shared editor with a driver and a live reviewer is both at once. B is wrong: documentation should still be written throughout development (CRD-2.G.3); watching code being typed leaves nothing for future maintainers. C is wrong: collaboration reduces mistakes but guarantees nothing; errors are still found through testing and debugging (CRD-2.I.5). D is wrong: testing uses defined inputs and expected outcomes (CRD-2.J.1); reading code is not a substitute for running it.

questions.stem: GalleryGo, a museum's touchscreen kiosk program, sits idle at a menu until a visitor taps an exhibit icon; the tap immediately starts a video about that exhibit regardless of what the kiosk was doing. Which statement BEST describes how this program operates?
options: The program executes its statements strictly top to bottom, so the video plays only after every menu statement has finished running
The tap is an output produced by the program, since it directly changes what appears on the kiosk's screen
The tap is an event that supplies input data to the program, and in event-driven programming statements execute when triggered rather than through sequential flow of control
The program can accept input only at the moment it is first started, so each tap must restart the entire program from the beginning
questions.rationale: CRD-2.C.2 says an event is associated with an action and supplies input data to a program, and CRD-2.C.5 says that in event-driven programming, statements execute when triggered rather than through sequential flow. The tap is the triggering event. A is wrong: it describes sequential flow of control, which the scenario contradicts since the video interrupts the idle menu. B is wrong: the tap is data sent TO the program (input, CRD-2.C.1); the video is the output. D is wrong: events can be generated at any time a defined action occurs (CRD-2.C.3), not only at program start.

questions.stem: FareTap card readers on a city's buses respond when a rider taps a transit card. Two riders tap identical card models one after the other: the first reader beeps and shows GO, while the second shows ADD VALUE and stays locked. Which explanation is MOST consistent with the CED's description of program output?
options: Program output is usually based on input and prior state, so the different stored balances on the two cards produce different outputs
Program output is unrelated to program input, so the two readers' different responses are effectively random behavior
The two readers must be running unrelated programs, since a single program cannot produce two different outputs
The second reader encountered a syntax error that was triggered by the low balance stored on the rider's card
questions.rationale: CRD-2.D.2 says program output is usually based on a program's input or prior state (such as internal values), and CRD-2.C.4 says inputs usually affect output; the same program reads different card balances and produces different outputs. B is wrong: it directly contradicts CRD-2.C.4. C is wrong: a program must work for a variety of inputs and situations (CRD-2.B.3), producing different outputs for different inputs by design. D is wrong: a syntax error is a violation of the programming language's rules (CRD-2.I.2); it is not caused by the data a running program reads.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly describes the relationship between a program and a code segment?
options: A code segment is the physical hardware component inside a computer on which a program's statements are permanently stored
A program and a code segment are interchangeable terms, and either one can describe an entire piece of software
A code segment must be a complete program that runs on its own, while a program is a fragment that cannot run by itself
A program is a collection of program statements that performs a specific task when run, and a code segment is a collection of statements that is part of a program
questions.rationale: CRD-2.B.1 defines a program (often called software) as a collection of program statements that performs a specific task when run by a computer, and CRD-2.B.2 defines a code segment as a collection of program statements that is part of a program. A is wrong: both terms describe statements, not hardware; a program is software. B is wrong: the terms are not interchangeable; a code segment is a part of a program, not the whole. C is wrong: it reverses the definitions; the program is the whole and the code segment is the part.

questions.stem: A volunteer group asks a student team to 'build something to help the food pantry.' Before designing anything, the team interviews pantry staff and observes clients picking up orders, and learns the real problem is that donated items expire unrecorded on the shelves. The team then defines PantryLink, an app whose purpose is tracking expiration dates. According to the CED, why was this early work valuable?
options: It allowed the team to skip the design and prototyping phases entirely and move straight to writing the final version of the app
Understanding a computing innovation's purpose improves the ability to develop it, and investigation identifies program constraints and the concerns of the people who will use the program
It guaranteed that the finished app would require no testing phase, since the intended users had already been consulted in person
It eliminated the need to write program requirements, since direct observation of users can take the place of a specification
questions.ek: EK CRD-2.A.2 + EK CRD-2.F.2 + EK CRD-2.F.3
questions.rationale: CRD-2.A.2 says understanding the purpose of a computing innovation gives developers an improved ability to develop it, and CRD-2.F.2 says investigation identifies program constraints and the concerns and interests of the people who will use the program; interviews and direct observations are named investigation methods (CRD-2.F.3). A is wrong: investigation comes before and informs design; it does not replace design or prototyping (CRD-2.E.2). C is wrong: testing with defined inputs is still required to confirm expected outcomes (CRD-2.J.1). D is wrong: investigation produces the understanding used to write requirements and the specification (CRD-2.F.1, CRD-2.F.5); it does not replace them.

questions.stem: The RideRing team is building a carpool scheduler. The team completes the ride-matching feature and verifies it works before starting the notifications feature, and after early users report that the matching screen is confusing, the team returns to the design phase and reworks it. Which pair of terms BEST characterizes this development process?
options: Ordered and exploratory, since the team wrote the entire program before testing any part of it
Event-driven and modular, since each new feature responds to a different action from the app's users
Incremental and iterative, since the problem is broken into pieces that are verified one at a time, and feedback sends the team back to an earlier phase for revision
Random and trial-based, since revisiting an earlier phase of development shows the team never had a plan
questions.rationale: CRD-2.E.4 defines an incremental process as one that breaks the problem into smaller pieces and makes sure each piece works before adding it to the whole (matching feature verified before notifications), and CRD-2.E.3 defines an iterative process as one requiring refinement and revision based on feedback, testing, or reflection, which may mean revisiting earlier phases (returning to design after user feedback). A is wrong: the team tested piece by piece, not all at the end, and 'ordered' versus 'exploratory' describes how a process is planned (CRD-2.E.1), not this pattern. B is wrong: event-driven describes how program statements are triggered during execution (CRD-2.C.5), not a development process. D is wrong: revisiting earlier phases is a defining feature of a deliberate iterative process, not evidence of having no plan.

questions.stem: The design notes for PlotShare, a community-garden reservation app, contain this line: 'When a gardener selects an open plot, the program must display a confirmation and send a message to the garden coordinator.' What does this line represent?
options: A logic error report, since it describes program behavior that has not yet been implemented in code
Program documentation, since it explains how an already completed code segment was developed
A program input, since it is text that users of the app will send to the program for processing
A program requirement, since it describes how the program functions, including a user interaction the program must provide
questions.rationale: CRD-2.F.4 says program requirements describe how a program functions and may include a description of the user interactions the program must provide; per CRD-2.F.5, the specification defines these requirements. A is wrong: a logic error is a mistake causing incorrect behavior in an existing algorithm or program (CRD-2.I.1); unimplemented planned behavior is not an error. B is wrong: documentation describes the function of code and how it was developed (CRD-2.G.1); this line prescribes future behavior rather than describing built code. C is wrong: inputs are data sent to the program for processing while it runs (CRD-2.C.1); this sentence is part of the plan, not data the program processes.

questions.stem: Midway through building ChoirFolder, an app that organizes sheet music for a chorus, the lead developer leaves the project. A replacement developer joins and becomes productive within days, locating the purpose and history of each procedure without outside help. Which practice MOST directly explains the smooth handoff?
options: The team wrote no comments at all, which kept the code shorter and therefore faster for the new developer to read
The team documented the program throughout its development, which helps in developing and maintaining correct programs when working collaboratively
The team planned to write all of the project's documentation in one single session after the app was released
The team relied on the computer to infer each procedure's purpose automatically whenever the program was executed
questions.rationale: CRD-2.G.3 says programmers should document a program throughout its development, and CRD-2.G.4 says documentation helps in developing and maintaining correct programs when working individually or collaboratively; up-to-date descriptions of each procedure are exactly what lets a newcomer get oriented. A is wrong: comments are documentation written for people and do not affect how a program runs (CRD-2.G.2), so removing them saves nothing at execution and destroys the record the new developer needed. C is wrong: documentation deferred to the end would not exist midway through the project, when the handoff happened. D is wrong: documentation is a written description created by people (CRD-2.G.1); running a program does not generate explanations of each procedure's purpose and history.

questions.stem: For the maze game MazeMoth, a student uses a collision-detection procedure written by a classmate and a color-palette function taken from an open-source project. To meet the CED's expectation for acknowledging code from other sources, what must the student's program documentation include?
options: The origin or the original author's name for each code segment that was written by someone else
A note for the open-source function only, since code written collaboratively with a classmate does not require acknowledgment
A rewritten version of both code segments, since code written by others may never appear inside a student's program
The total number of lines the student personally wrote, so that a reader can compute what percentage of the code is original
questions.rationale: CRD-2.H.1 says it is important to acknowledge any code segments developed collaboratively or by another source, and CRD-2.H.2 says the acknowledgment can appear in the program documentation and should include the origin or original author's name. B is wrong: CRD-2.H.1 explicitly covers collaboratively developed code, so the classmate's procedure must be acknowledged too. C is wrong: using others' code is acceptable when acknowledged; nothing requires rewriting it. D is wrong: the CED asks for attribution of the borrowed segments' origin, not a line count or an originality percentage.

questions.stem: The CampCart team codes a camp-store checkout program without writing a specification or planning how the program will be evaluated. When volunteers later try the program, no one can say whether any output is wrong, since nothing was ever recorded about what the program is supposed to produce. Which design-phase step was MOST directly skipped?
options: Creating diagrams that represent the layouts of the program's user-interface screens
Organizing the program's code into separate modules and functional components
Developing a testing strategy for the program, since program requirements are needed to identify appropriate defined inputs and their expected outcomes
Brainstorming a wide range of alternative solutions before committing to a single design
questions.rationale: CRD-2.F.7 lists development of a testing strategy as part of the design phase, and CRD-2.J.3 says program requirements are needed to identify appropriate defined inputs for testing; with no specification and no testing plan, the volunteers have no expected outcomes to compare against. A is wrong: interface diagrams are a design activity, but their absence does not explain why outputs cannot be judged right or wrong. B is wrong: missing modularity affects code organization, not the ability to know expected outcomes. D is wrong: skipping brainstorming limits the exploration of options, but the described failure is specifically the absence of requirements-based expected outcomes.

questions.stem: BadgeBoard, a scout troop's badge tracker, runs from start to finish without any error messages, but leaders notice it marks a badge complete when a scout has finished only four of the five required activities. The team fixes the faulty comparison and then, following its development process, returns to an earlier phase to re-verify the related features. Which classification and response does the CED support?
options: A syntax error was present, and the team should have waited for the programming language itself to flag the faulty line
A run-time error was present, since every mistake in a program must eventually halt execution with an error message
An overflow error was present, since the number of completed activities exceeded the range the computer can store
A logic error was present, since the program ran but behaved incorrectly, and revisiting earlier phases based on testing is characteristic of an iterative process
questions.rationale: CRD-2.I.1 defines a logic error as a mistake in the algorithm or program that causes it to behave incorrectly or unexpectedly; the tracker runs cleanly but produces wrong results, the hallmark of a logic error. Returning to earlier phases after testing reveals a problem matches the iterative process in CRD-2.E.3. A is wrong: a syntax error breaks the language's rules (CRD-2.I.2) and would prevent the program from running normally, but this program runs. B is wrong: a run-time error occurs during execution with the language reporting it (CRD-2.I.3); logic errors produce wrong answers silently. C is wrong: an overflow error involves a number outside the defined range of values (CRD-2.I.4); a count of four activities is nowhere near any storage limit.

questions.stem: A development team triages its bug tracker and classifies three reports: I. A missing closing brace violates the programming language's rules, so the program will not run; the team labels it a syntax error. II. The program runs to completion but computes order totals incorrectly; the team labels it a logic error. III. A membership counter is assigned a number outside the range of values its storage can represent; the team labels it an overflow error. Which of the classifications are correct?
options: I, II, and III
I and II only
II and III only
I and III only
questions.ek: EK CRD-2.I.1 + EK CRD-2.I.2 + EK CRD-2.I.4
questions.rationale: All three labels match the CED taxonomy: a mistake where the rules of the language are not followed is a syntax error (CRD-2.I.2); a mistake that causes incorrect or unexpected behavior while the program runs is a logic error (CRD-2.I.1); and a number outside the defined range of values a computer can handle is an overflow error (CRD-2.I.4). B is wrong: it rejects III, but the counter case is the CED's definition of overflow. C is wrong: it rejects I, but a rule-of-the-language violation is exactly a syntax error. D is wrong: it rejects II, but a program that runs yet computes wrong totals is the definition of a logic error.

questions.stem: LaserLap records lap counts at a go-kart track; its lap-counter variable has a defined maximum value of 65,535. The current test plan uses lap counts of 10, 500, and 1,000, and every test passes. Which change BEST addresses the risk this plan misses, and which error type does the change target?
options: Add mid-range inputs such as 20,000 and 30,000, targeting a syntax error that appears only at those specific values
Add inputs at and just beyond the extremes, such as 65,535 and 65,536, targeting an overflow error when the count passes the defined range of values
Remove the input 10 from the plan, since very small values cannot contribute useful information to testing
Rerun the same three passing inputs several more times, targeting a run-time error that appears only after repetition
questions.rationale: CRD-2.J.2 says defined test inputs should demonstrate expected outcomes at or just beyond the extremes of the input data, and CRD-2.I.4 defines overflow as a number outside the defined range of values; testing 65,535 and 65,536 is exactly the boundary probe the current mid-range-only plan lacks. A is wrong: syntax errors are violations of language rules (CRD-2.I.2) and do not depend on input values at run time. C is wrong: extremes at the low end matter too; removing coverage weakens the plan rather than extending it toward the untested boundary. D is wrong: repeating identical passing inputs exercises nothing new; the risk lies past the maximum, not in repetition.

partII.instructions: Answer in complete sentences; use precise development-process, documentation, and error vocabulary from Big Idea 1.

questions.prompt: A three-student team is building SeedSwap, an app that catalogs a community seed library and matches gardeners who want to trade seeds. The team begins by interviewing gardeners about how trades happen now, sketches the main screens on paper, releases a rough working version to five gardeners, and reworks the trade-matching screen twice based on their feedback. From the first week, the team writes comments describing what each procedure does and why it was written that way. The finished program also includes a QR-code-scanning procedure the team downloaded from an open-source project.
parts: (a) Using the phases commonly used when developing a program, explain how the team's process is iterative.
(b) Explain TWO ways the team's habit of documenting the program throughout development benefits the team.
(c) Describe what the team must do to properly acknowledge the QR-code-scanning procedure, including where the acknowledgment can appear and what it must contain.
sample: (a) The team's work moves through the commonly used development phases: interviewing gardeners is investigating and reflecting, sketching screens is designing, releasing a rough working version is prototyping, and giving it to five gardeners to use is testing (CRD-2.E.2). The process is iterative because the team refines and revises based on feedback: after gardeners tested the prototype, the team returned to the design phase and reworked the trade-matching screen twice. Revisiting earlier phases in response to feedback, testing, or reflection is exactly what makes a development process iterative (CRD-2.E.3).

(b) First, documentation written throughout development helps the team develop and maintain a correct program while collaborating: any of the three students can read the comments to understand what a procedure does and why, instead of relying on the person who wrote it (CRD-2.G.4). Second, documenting as they go, rather than reconstructing explanations after release, follows the CED's guidance that programmers should document a program throughout its development (CRD-2.G.3); the comments record decisions while they are fresh, and since comments are written for people and do not affect how the program runs (CRD-2.G.2), there is no performance cost to keeping them.

(c) Because the QR-code-scanning procedure was written by another source, the team must acknowledge it (CRD-2.H.1). The acknowledgment can be placed in the program documentation, such as a comment near the procedure, and it must identify the origin of the code or the original author's name, for example naming the open-source project the procedure came from (CRD-2.H.2).
rubric: 2 pts: Part (a) names the commonly used phases (investigating and reflecting, designing, prototyping, testing) and maps at least three of them to the team's actions (CRD-2.E.2).

1 pt: Part (a) explains that the process is iterative: refinement and revision based on feedback, testing, or reflection, including revisiting earlier phases, shown by the twice-reworked matching screen (CRD-2.E.3).

1 pt: Part (b) shows understanding of what documentation is: a written description of the function of a code segment, event, procedure, or program and how it was developed; comments are read by people and do not affect execution (CRD-2.G.1, CRD-2.G.2).

2 pts: Part (b) gives two distinct benefits, such as developing and maintaining correct programs in a collaborative environment (CRD-2.G.4) and documenting throughout development rather than at the end (CRD-2.G.3). 1 pt per benefit.

2 pts: Part (c) states that borrowed code must be acknowledged (CRD-2.H.1), that the acknowledgment can appear in the program documentation, and that it must include the origin or original author's name (CRD-2.H.2).
questions.ek: EK CRD-2.E.2 + EK CRD-2.E.3 + EK CRD-2.G.1 + EK CRD-2.G.2 + EK CRD-2.G.3 + EK CRD-2.G.4 + EK CRD-2.H.1 + EK CRD-2.H.2

questions.prompt: RinkPass sells tickets for public ice-skating sessions. Its program requirements state that a booking must contain from 1 to 40 skaters and that groups of 6 or more receive a group discount. Three problems are reported: (1) after an edit, the program will not run at all, and the editor points to a line with a missing closing parenthesis; (2) the program runs, but a group of exactly 6 skaters is charged full price instead of the group rate; (3) the running program halts with an error message when a booking form is submitted with the skater-count field left empty.
parts: (a) Classify each of the three reported problems using the CED's error types, and justify each classification.
(b) Using the requirements, describe a set of defined test inputs for the skater count that follows the CED's guidance about extremes, and state the expected outcome for each input.
(c) Describe TWO effective methods from the CED that the team could use to find and correct problem 2, and explain how each method would reveal the error.
sample: (a) Problem 1 is a syntax error: a missing closing parenthesis means the rules of the programming language are not followed, which is why the program cannot run (CRD-2.I.2). Problem 2 is a logic error: the program runs without complaint but behaves incorrectly, charging a 6-skater group full price when the requirements say 6 or more get the discount, so the mistake is in the algorithm's comparison (CRD-2.I.1). Problem 3 is a run-time error: the mistake appears during the execution of the program, which halts with an error message when it tries to process the empty field (CRD-2.I.3).

(b) The requirements say bookings hold 1 to 40 skaters, and program requirements are what identify appropriate defined inputs (CRD-2.J.3). Test inputs should demonstrate the different expected outcomes at or just beyond the extremes (CRD-2.J.2): 0 skaters (just beyond the minimum, expected outcome: booking rejected), 1 skater (at the minimum, expected: accepted at full price), 40 skaters (at the maximum, expected: accepted with the group discount), and 41 skaters (just beyond the maximum, expected: rejected). Because the discount boundary is also part of the requirements, 5 skaters (expected: full price) and 6 skaters (expected: group rate) should be tested too. The results from these tests are then used to revise the program (CRD-2.J.1).

(c) One effective method is running test cases at the discount boundary: testing 5, 6, and 7 skaters against the expected prices shows immediately that 6 produces the wrong outcome, isolating the faulty comparison (CRD-2.I.5, CRD-2.J.1). A second method is hand tracing the pricing code with the value 6: stepping through each statement and writing down variable values reveals whether the comparison uses 'greater than 6' where the requirements demand 'greater than or equal to 6' (CRD-2.I.5). Adding extra output statements that display the skater count and which price branch executed, or stepping through with a debugger, would expose the same faulty branch.
rubric: 3 pts: Part (a) correctly classifies and justifies all three: syntax error, the language's rules are not followed (CRD-2.I.2); logic error, the program runs but behaves incorrectly (CRD-2.I.1); run-time error, the mistake occurs during program execution (CRD-2.I.3). 1 pt per correct classification with justification.

2 pts: Part (b) chooses inputs at and just beyond the extremes of the 1-to-40 requirement, such as 0, 1, 40, and 41 (CRD-2.J.2), and notes that the requirements are what identify appropriate defined inputs (CRD-2.J.3).
1 pt: Part (b) states an expected outcome for each defined input and indicates that test results are used to revise the program (CRD-2.J.1).
2 pts: Part (c) names two distinct methods from CRD-2.I.5 (test cases, hand tracing, visualizations, debuggers, adding extra output statements). 1 pt per method.
1 pt: Part (c) explains how at least one chosen method exposes the faulty boundary comparison at 6 skaters (e.g., hand tracing the comparison with the value 6, or a boundary test case at 5/6/7).
questions.ek: EK CRD-2.I.1 + EK CRD-2.I.2 + EK CRD-2.I.3 + EK CRD-2.I.5 + EK CRD-2.J.1 + EK CRD-2.J.2 + EK CRD-2.J.3
